State the precise (\(\varepsilon\)–\(\delta\)) definition of \(\lim_{x\to a} f(x) = L\).
column2: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that if \(0 < |x - a| < \delta\) then \(|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon\).

column1: When is \(f\) continuous at \(x = a\)?
column2: \(f\) is continuous at \(a\) if \(f(a)\) is defined, \(\lim_{x\to a} f(x)\) exists, and \(\lim_{x\to a} f(x) = f(a)\).

column1: Evaluate \(\lim_{x\to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}\).
Evaluate \(\lim_{x\to 0} \frac{1 - \cos x}{x}\).
State the limit definition of the derivative \(f'(x)\).
column2: \(f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}\).

column1: Product rule: \(\frac{d}{dx}[f(x)g(x)]\) = ?
Quotient rule: \(\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right]\) = ?
Chain rule: \(\frac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))]\) = ?
column2: \(f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)\)

column1: \(\frac{d}{dx}[\tan x]\) = ?
\(\frac{d}{dx}[\ln x]\) = ?
\(\frac{d}{dx}[\arctan x]\) = ?
\(\frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin x]\) = ?
State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1).
column2: If \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\) and \(F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt\), then \(F'(x) = f(x)\).

column1: State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 2, evaluation).
column2: If \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\) and \(F\) is any antiderivative of \(f\), then \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)\).

column1: Integration by parts formula: \(\int u\,dv\) = ?
column2: \(uv - \int v\,du\)

column1: \(\int \frac{1}{x}\,dx\) = ?

column2: \(\tan x + C\)
\(\arctan x + C\)

column1: Evaluate \(\int x e^{x}\,dx\).
Sum of a convergent geometric series \(\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a r^{n}\) (\(|r| < 1\)) = ?
For which \(p\) does the \(p\)-series \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{p}}\) converge?
column2: It converges if and only if \(p > 1\).

column1: State the Ratio Test conclusion using \(L = \lim_{n\to\infty} \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right|\).
column2: If \(L < 1\) the series converges absolutely; if \(L > 1\) (or \(\infty\)) it diverges; if \(L = 1\) the test is inconclusive.

column1: State the Alternating Series Test.
column2: \(\sum (-1)^{n} b_n\) converges if \(b_n > 0\), \(b_n\) is decreasing, and \(\lim_{n\to\infty} b_n = 0\).

column1: Maclaurin series of \(e^{x}\) = ?
Maclaurin series of \(\sin x\) = ?
Maclaurin series of \(\cos x\) = ?
Power series of \(\frac{1}{1 - x}\) for \(|x| < 1\) = ?
Define the gradient \(\nabla f\) of a scalar function \(f(x,y,z)\).
column2: \(\nabla f = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right)\), the vector of first partial derivatives.

column1: For \(z = f(x,y)\) with \(x = x(t)\), \(y = y(t)\), the chain rule gives \(\frac{dz}{dt}\) = ?
column2: \(\frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt}\)

column1: Give the area element \(dA\) for a double integral in polar coordinates.
column2: \(dA = r\,dr\,d\theta\)

column1: State the Jacobian volume element for spherical coordinates \((\rho, \varphi, \theta)\).
column2: \(dV = \rho^{2} \sin\varphi\,d\rho\,d\varphi\,d\theta\)

column1: State Green's Theorem.
column2: \(\oint_C (P\,dx + Q\,dy) = \iint_D \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\right) dA\), where \(C\) is the positively oriented boundary of region \(D\).

column1: State the Divergence Theorem.
column2: \(\iint_S F \cdot dS = \iiint_V (\nabla \cdot F)\,dV\), where \(S\) is the closed boundary surface of solid region \(V\).

column1: State Stokes' Theorem.
column2: \(\oint_C F \cdot dr = \iint_S (\nabla \times F) \cdot dS\), where \(C\) is the boundary of surface \(S\) with compatible orientation.

column1: Give the arc length of \(y = f(x)\) from \(x = a\) to \(x = b\).
Volume of revolution about the \(x\)-axis (disk method) for \(y = f(x)\), \(a \le x \le b\) = ?
Solve the separable ODE \(\frac{dy}{dx} = k y\).
Give the integrating factor for the linear ODE \(y' + P(x) y = Q(x)\).
column2: \(\mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx}\)

column1: For a \(2\times 2\) matrix \(A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\), what is \(\det(A)\)?
column2: \(\det(A) = ad - bc\)

column1: How do you expand \(\det\) of a \(3\times 3\) matrix \(\begin{pmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i \end{pmatrix}\) along the first row?
column2: \(\det = a(ei - fh) - b(di - fg) + c(dh - eg)\)

column1: When is a square matrix \(A\) invertible? Give several equivalent conditions.
column2: \(A\) is invertible \(\iff \det(A) \ne 0 \iff\) rank \(= n\) (full rank) \(\iff\) columns are linearly independent \(\iff 0\) is not an eigenvalue \(\iff Ax = 0\) has only the trivial solution

column1: State the rank-nullity theorem for a linear map \(T: V \to W\) with \(V\) finite-dimensional.
column2: \(\dim(V) = \operatorname{rank}(T) + \operatorname{nullity}(T)\), i.e. \(\dim(V) = \dim(\operatorname{im} T) + \dim(\ker T)\)

column1: What is a basis of a vector space, and what is the dimension?
column2: A basis is a linearly independent set that spans the space; the dimension is the number of vectors in any basis (all bases have the same size)

column1: Define an eigenvalue and eigenvector of a matrix \(A\).
column2: A nonzero vector \(v\) with \(Av = \lambda v\) for a scalar \(\lambda\); \(\lambda\) is the eigenvalue and \(v\) is an eigenvector

column1: How is the characteristic polynomial of an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\) defined?
column2: \(p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)\); its roots are the eigenvalues of \(A\)

column1: When is an \(n\times n\) matrix diagonalizable (over a field)?
column2: \(A\) is diagonalizable \(\iff\) it has \(n\) linearly independent eigenvectors \(\iff\) for every eigenvalue its geometric multiplicity equals its algebraic multiplicity

column1: Relate trace and determinant of \(A\) to its eigenvalues (with multiplicity).
column2: \(\operatorname{trace}(A)\) = sum of eigenvalues; \(\det(A)\) = product of eigenvalues

column1: Is matrix multiplication commutative? State the rule for when \(AB\) is defined.
column2: No, in general \(AB \ne BA\); \(AB\) is defined when \(A\) is \(m\times n\) and \(B\) is \(n\times p\), giving an \(m\times p\) matrix

column1: How do elementary row operations affect a determinant?
column2: Swapping two rows multiplies \(\det\) by \(-1\); scaling a row by \(k\) multiplies \(\det\) by \(k\); adding a multiple of one row to another leaves \(\det\) unchanged

column1: When are two vectors \(u\) and \(v\) orthogonal?
column2: When their dot product is zero: \(u \cdot v = 0\)

column1: Give the cross product formula for \(a = (a_1,a_2,a_3)\) and \(b = (b_1,b_2,b_3)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^{3}\).
column2: \(a \times b = (a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2, a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3, a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1)\); it is orthogonal to both \(a\) and \(b\)

column1: Give the dot product formula and its geometric meaning.
column2: \(u \cdot v = u_1 v_1 + u_2 v_2 + \cdots + u_n v_n = |u|\,|v| \cos\theta\), where \(\theta\) is the angle between \(u\) and \(v\)

column1: What is the idea behind the Gram-Schmidt process?
column2: It converts a linearly independent set into an orthogonal (or orthonormal) basis by subtracting from each vector its projections onto the previously constructed vectors

column1: For \(Ax = b\), how do \(\operatorname{rank}(A)\) and \(\operatorname{rank}([A|b])\) determine solvability?
column2: Consistent \(\iff \operatorname{rank}(A) = \operatorname{rank}([A|b])\); then unique solution if rank = number of unknowns, infinitely many if rank < number of unknowns

column1: What three properties must a set \(G\) with operation \(\cdot\) satisfy to be a group?
column2: Associativity, existence of an identity element \(e\), and existence of an inverse for every element (closure under \(\cdot\) is also assumed)

column1: Define the order of a group and the order of an element \(g\).
column2: The order of a group is its number of elements \(|G|\); the order of \(g\) is the smallest positive integer \(n\) with \(g^{n} = e\) (\(\infty\) if none exists)

column1: State the subgroup test for a nonempty subset \(H\) of a group \(G\).
column2: \(H \le G \iff H\) is nonempty and for all \(a, b \in H\), \(ab^{-1} \in H\)

column1: State Lagrange's theorem.
column2: If \(G\) is a finite group and \(H \le G\), then \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\); in particular the order of any element divides \(|G|\)

column1: What is a cyclic group?
column2: A group generated by a single element: \(G = \langle g \rangle = \{g^{k} : k \in \mathbb{Z}\}\); every cyclic group is abelian

column1: What is a (left) coset of a subgroup \(H\) in \(G\)?
column2: For \(g \in G\), the set \(gH = \{gh : h \in H\}\); the distinct left cosets partition \(G\) into pieces of equal size \(|H|\)

column1: When is a subgroup \(N\) of \(G\) normal?
column2: \(N \trianglelefteq G \iff gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\) (equivalently \(gN = Ng\) for all \(g\))

column1: What is the quotient group \(G/N\), and what condition on \(N\) is needed?
column2: When \(N \trianglelefteq G\), \(G/N\) is the group of cosets with \((aN)(bN) = (ab)N\); \(N\) must be normal for this to be well-defined

column1: Define a group homomorphism \(\varphi: G \to H\).
column2: A map with \(\varphi(ab) = \varphi(a)\varphi(b)\) for all \(a, b \in G\); it sends identity to identity and inverses to inverses

column1: What is a group isomorphism?
column2: A bijective homomorphism; if one exists, \(G\) and \(H\) are isomorphic (\(G \cong H\)) and share all group-theoretic properties

column1: Define the kernel of a homomorphism \(\varphi: G \to H\), and its key properties.
column2: \(\ker(\varphi) = \{g \in G : \varphi(g) = e_H\}\); it is a normal subgroup of \(G\), and \(\varphi\) is injective \(\iff \ker(\varphi) = \{e\}\)

column1: What is the symmetric group \(S_n\) and its order?
column2: \(S_n\) is the group of all bijections (permutations) of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) under composition; \(|S_n| = n!\)

column1: What determines the parity (even/odd) of a permutation?
column2: The parity of the number of transpositions in any decomposition; even permutations form the alternating group \(A_n\) of order \(n!/2\)

column1: What are the defining properties of a ring (with unity)?
column2: \((R, +)\) is an abelian group, multiplication is associative and distributes over addition, and there is a multiplicative identity \(1\)

column1: Define an ideal \(I\) of a commutative ring \(R\).
column2: A subgroup \((I, +)\) such that for all \(r \in R\) and \(a \in I\), \(ra \in I\) (absorbs multiplication by ring elements)

column1: What is an integral domain?
column2: A commutative ring with \(1 \ne 0\) that has no zero divisors: \(ab = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0\) or \(b = 0\)

column1: What is a field?
column2: A commutative ring with \(1 \ne 0\) in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse

column1: For which \(n\) is the ring \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) a field?
column2: Exactly when \(n\) is prime; \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is an integral domain \(\iff n\) is prime, and then it is also a field

column1: What does \(a \mid b\) (\(a\) divides \(b\)) mean for integers?
column2: There exists an integer \(k\) with \(b = ak\) (and \(a \ne 0\))

column1: State the relationship between gcd and lcm of positive integers \(a\) and \(b\).
column2: \(\gcd(a,b) \cdot \operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = a \cdot b\)

column1: How does the Euclidean algorithm compute \(\gcd(a, b)\)?
column2: Repeatedly replace \((a, b)\) by \((b, a \bmod b)\) until the remainder is \(0\); the last nonzero remainder is \(\gcd(a, b)\)

column1: What does \(a \equiv b \pmod{n}\) mean?
column2: \(n\) divides \((a - b)\); equivalently \(a\) and \(b\) leave the same remainder upon division by \(n\)

column1: State Fermat's little theorem.
column2: If \(p\) is prime and \(\gcd(a, p) = 1\), then \(a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}\); for any \(a\), \(a^{p} \equiv a \pmod{p}\)

column1: Define Euler's totient function \(\varphi(n)\) and give \(\varphi\) for a prime \(p\) and a prime power \(p^{k}\).
column2: \(\varphi(n)\) counts integers in \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) coprime to \(n\); \(\varphi(p) = p - 1\) and \(\varphi(p^{k}) = p^{k} - p^{k-1}\)

column1: State Euler's theorem.
column2: If \(\gcd(a, n) = 1\), then \(a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}\)

column1: State the Chinese Remainder Theorem for two moduli.
column2: If \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\), the system \(x \equiv a \pmod{m}\), \(x \equiv b \pmod{n}\) has a unique solution modulo \(mn\)

column1: State the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.
column2: Every integer \(n > 1\) factors into primes uniquely up to the order of the factors

column1: Compute \(7^{100} \bmod 5\).
Compute \(2^{10} \bmod 11\) using Fermat's little theorem.
column2: By Fermat, \(2^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11}\) since \(11\) is prime and \(\gcd(2,11) = 1\)

column1: State the \(\varepsilon\)–\(\delta\) definition of \(\lim_{x\to a} f(x) = L\).
column2: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that for all \(x\), if \(0 < |x - a| < \delta\) then \(|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon\).

column1: State the \(\varepsilon\)–\(\delta\) definition of continuity of \(f\) at a point \(a\).
column2: \(f\) is continuous at \(a\) if for every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that for all \(x\), \(|x - a| < \delta\) implies \(|f(x) - f(a)| < \varepsilon\).

column1: What does it mean for a sequence \((a_n)\) to converge to \(L\)?
column2: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(N\) such that for all \(n \ge N\), \(|a_n - L| < \varepsilon\).

column1: Define a Cauchy sequence.
column2: \((a_n)\) is Cauchy if for every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(N\) such that for all \(m, n \ge N\), \(|a_m - a_n| < \varepsilon\).

column1: What is meant by completeness of \(\mathbb{R}\), and how does it relate to Cauchy sequences?
column2: \(\mathbb{R}\) is complete: every Cauchy sequence of real numbers converges to a limit in \(\mathbb{R}\). Equivalently, every nonempty set bounded above has a least upper bound.

column1: State the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem.
column2: Every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) (or \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\)) has a convergent subsequence.

column1: Define the supremum (sup) of a nonempty set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) that is bounded above.
column2: \(\sup S\) is the least upper bound of \(S\): it is an upper bound \(M\) such that no number smaller than \(M\) is an upper bound. That is, \(M\) is an upper bound and for every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there is \(s \in S\) with \(s > M - \varepsilon\).

column1: Distinguish pointwise from uniform convergence of \(f_n \to f\) on a set \(E\).
column2: Pointwise: for each \(x \in E\) and each \(\varepsilon > 0\) there is \(N\) (depending on \(x\)) with \(|f_n(x) - f(x)| < \varepsilon\) for \(n \ge N\). Uniform: \(N\) can be chosen independent of \(x\), so \(\sup\) over \(x\) of \(|f_n(x) - f(x)| \to 0\).

column1: State the nth-term test (divergence test) for a series \(\sum a_n\).
column2: If \(\sum a_n\) converges then \(a_n \to 0\). Contrapositive: if \(a_n\) does not tend to \(0\), the series diverges. (The converse fails, e.g. \(\sum \frac{1}{n}\).)

column1: State the Intermediate Value Theorem.
column2: If \(f\) is continuous on \([a, b]\) and \(y\) is any value between \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\), then there exists \(c \in [a, b]\) with \(f(c) = y\).

column1: State the Extreme Value Theorem.
column2: If \(f\) is continuous on a closed bounded interval \([a, b]\), then \(f\) attains a maximum and a minimum value on \([a, b]\).

column1: State the Mean Value Theorem.
column2: If \(f\) is continuous on \([a, b]\) and differentiable on \((a, b)\), then there exists \(c \in (a, b)\) with \(f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}\).

column1: Is differentiability at a point stronger or weaker than continuity there? Give the implication.
column2: If \(f\) is differentiable at \(a\), then \(f\) is continuous at \(a\). The converse is false (e.g. \(f(x) = |x|\) at \(0\) is continuous but not differentiable).

column1: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem for sequences.
column2: A monotone (increasing or decreasing) sequence of real numbers converges if and only if it is bounded; an increasing bounded sequence converges to its supremum.

column1: Give a criterion for Riemann integrability on \([a, b]\) in terms of upper and lower sums.
column2: A bounded \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \([a, b]\) iff for every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there is a partition \(P\) with \(U(f, P) - L(f, P) < \varepsilon\) (upper and lower Darboux sums differ by less than \(\varepsilon\)).

column1: Define an open set in a topological space.
column2: A set \(U\) is open if \(U\) belongs to the topology; equivalently in a metric space, for every point \(x \in U\) there is \(r > 0\) with the open ball \(B(x, r) \subseteq U\).

column1: Define a closed set in a topological space.
column2: A set is closed if its complement is open. Equivalently, it contains all of its limit points.

column1: Define the interior, closure, and boundary of a set \(A\).
column2: Interior: largest open set contained in \(A\) (union of all open subsets). Closure: smallest closed set containing \(A\) (intersection of all closed supersets). Boundary: closure(A) \(\cap\) closure(complement of A), i.e. closure minus interior.

column1: Define a basis for a topology on \(X\).
column2: A collection \(\mathcal{B}\) of open sets is a basis if every open set is a union of members of \(\mathcal{B}\); equivalently, for each \(x\) and open \(U\) containing \(x\) there is \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) with \(x \in B \subseteq U\).

column1: Give the topological characterization of continuity of \(f : X \to Y\).
column2: \(f\) is continuous iff the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) is open in \(X\) for every open set \(V \subseteq Y\) (equivalently, preimages of closed sets are closed).

column1: Define compactness of a topological space.
column2: A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover: for any collection of open sets whose union is \(X\), finitely many of them already cover \(X\).

column1: State the Heine–Borel theorem in \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\).
column2: A subset of \(\mathbb{R}^{n}\) is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

column1: Define a connected topological space.
column2: \(X\) is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets; equivalently, the only subsets that are both open and closed are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\).

column1: Define a Hausdorff (\(T_2\)) space.
column2:
A space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points \(x 
e y\) have disjoint open neighborhoods \(U 
i x\) and \(V 
i y\) with \(U \cap V = \emptyset\).

column1: Define a metric space.
column2: A set \(X\) with a function \(d : X \times X \to [0, \infty)\) such that \(d(x, y) = 0\) iff \(x = y\), \(d(x, y) = d(y, x)\) (symmetry), and \(d(x, z) \le d(x, y) + d(y, z)\) (triangle inequality).

column1: Define a homeomorphism between topological spaces.
column2: A bijection \(f : X \to Y\) such that both \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\) are continuous. If one exists, \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic (topologically equivalent).

column1: Is the open interval \((0, 1)\) compact in \(\mathbb{R}\)? Justify briefly.
column2: No. It is bounded but not closed, so by Heine–Borel it is not compact. E.g. the cover \(\{(1/n, 1) : n \ge 2\}\) has no finite subcover.

column1: Define a complex number's modulus \(|z|\) and argument \(\arg(z)\) for \(z = x + iy\).
column2: \(|z| = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}\), the distance from \(0\). \(\arg(z)\) is the angle \(\theta\) with \(x = |z|\cos\theta\), \(y = |z|\sin\theta\), so \(z = |z|(\cos\theta + i \sin\theta)\).

column1: State Euler's formula.
column2: \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i \sin\theta\). In particular \(e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0\).

column1: What are the nth roots of unity, and what do they look like geometrically?
column2: The solutions of \(z^{n} = 1\): \(z = e^{2\pi i k/n}\) for \(k = 0, 1, \dots, n - 1\). They are \(n\) equally spaced points on the unit circle \(|z| = 1\).

column1: State the Cauchy–Riemann equations for \(f = u + iv\) with \(z = x + iy\).
column2: If \(f\) is holomorphic then \(u_x = v_y\) and \(u_y = -v_x\) (partial derivatives). Together with continuous partials, these imply \(f\) is holomorphic.

column1: Define what it means for a function to be analytic (holomorphic) on an open set.
column2: \(f\) is holomorphic on an open set \(U\) if it is complex-differentiable at every point of \(U\), i.e. the limit \(\frac{f(z+h) - f(z)}{h}\) exists as \(h \to 0\) (\(h\) complex) for each \(z \in U\).

column1: State Cauchy's integral theorem.
column2: If \(f\) is holomorphic on a simply connected open set and \(\gamma\) is a closed contour in it, then \(\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0\).

column1: State Cauchy's integral formula.
column2: If \(f\) is holomorphic on and inside a positively oriented simple closed contour \(\gamma\), and \(a\) is interior to \(\gamma\), then \(f(a) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{z - a}\,dz\).

column1: State the residue theorem.
column2: If \(f\) is holomorphic inside and on a positively oriented simple closed contour \(\gamma\) except at isolated singularities \(z_k\), then \(\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \sum \operatorname{Res}(f, z_k)\).

column1: How do you compute the residue at a simple pole \(z_0\) of \(f\)?
column2: \(\operatorname{Res}(f, z_0) = \lim_{z\to z_0} (z - z_0) f(z)\). For \(f = g/h\) with simple zero of \(h\) at \(z_0\), this equals \(\frac{g(z_0)}{h'(z_0)}\).

column1: For \(z = x + iy\), give the complex conjugate \(\bar{z}\) and the identity relating \(z\), \(\bar{z}\), and \(|z|\).
column2: \(\bar{z} = x - iy\). Then \(z\cdot\bar{z} = x^{2} + y^{2} = |z|^{2}\), so \(|z| = \sqrt{z \bar{z}}\).

column1: Under the map \(w = e^{z}\), what is the image of a horizontal line \(y = c\) (\(c\) constant) in the \(z\)-plane?
column2: Writing \(z = x + ic\), \(w = e^{x} e^{ic}\), so as \(x\) ranges over \(\mathbb{R}\), \(w\) traces the ray from the origin (excluding \(0\)) at angle \(c\): \(\arg(w) = c\), \(|w| = e^{x} > 0\).

column1: What is a sample space?
column2: The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment, usually denoted \(\Omega\) or \(S\).

column1: State the three Kolmogorov axioms of probability.
column2: 1) \(P(A) \ge 0\) for any event \(A\); 2) \(P(\Omega) = 1\); 3) for disjoint events, \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\).

column1: Define the conditional probability \(P(A \mid B)\).
column2: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\), provided \(P(B) > 0\).

column1: State Bayes' theorem.
column2: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}\), where \(P(B) > 0\).

column1: When are two events \(A\) and \(B\) independent?
column2: When \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\); equivalently \(P(A \mid B) = P(A)\).

column1: State the law of total probability for a partition \(B_1,\dots,B_n\) of the sample space.
column2: \(P(A) = \sum P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)\) summed over \(i = 1\) to \(n\).

column1: State the linearity of expectation for random variables \(X\) and \(Y\).
column2: \(E[aX + bY] = a\cdot E[X] + b\cdot E[Y]\), which holds even if \(X\) and \(Y\) are not independent.

column1: Define the variance of a random variable \(X\).
column2: \(\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^{2}] = E[X^{2}] - (E[X])^{2}\), where \(\mu = E[X]\).

column1: What are the mean and variance of a Binomial\((n, p)\) distribution?
column2: Mean \(= np\) and variance \(= np(1 - p)\).

column1: State the 68-95-99.7 rule for a normal distribution.
column2: About 68% of values lie within \(1\sigma\) of \(\mu\), 95% within \(2\sigma\), and 99.7% within \(3\sigma\).

column1: What are the mean and variance of a Poisson\((\lambda)\) distribution?
column2: Both the mean and the variance equal \(\lambda\).

column1: For a geometric distribution with success probability \(p\) (trials until first success), what is the expected number of trials?
column2: \(E[X] = \frac{1}{p}\), and variance \(= \frac{1 - p}{p^{2}}\).

column1: When rolling two fair dice, what is \(P(\text{sum} = 7)\)?
column2: \(\frac{1}{6}\), since 6 of the 36 equally likely outcomes sum to 7.

column1: What is the mean of a continuous Uniform\((a, b)\) distribution?
column2: \(\frac{a + b}{2}\), and its variance is \(\frac{(b - a)^{2}}{12}\).

column1: State the fundamental counting (multiplication) principle.
column2: If one task can be done in \(m\) ways and another in \(n\) ways, the two together can be done in \(m \cdot n\) ways.

column1: How many permutations (orderings) are there of \(n\) distinct objects?
column2: \(n! = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2) \cdot \cdots \cdot 2 \cdot 1\).

column1: Give the formula for combinations \(C(n, k)\).
column2: \(C(n, k) = \frac{n!}{k! (n - k)!}\), the number of \(k\)-element subsets of an \(n\)-set.

column1: State the binomial theorem.
column2: \((x + y)^{n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n} C(n, k) \cdot x^{n-k} \cdot y^{k}\).

column1: State the pigeonhole principle.
column2: If \(n\) items are placed into \(m\) boxes with \(n > m\), then at least one box contains at least two items.

column1: State the inclusion-exclusion principle for two sets.
column2: \(|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|\).

column1: State the handshaking lemma (degree sum formula) for a graph.
column2: The sum of all vertex degrees equals \(2|E|\), twice the number of edges.

column1: How many edges does a tree with \(n\) vertices have?
column2: Exactly \(n - 1\) edges.

column1: State Euler's formula for a connected planar graph.
column2: \(V - E + F = 2\), where \(V\), \(E\), \(F\) are the numbers of vertices, edges, and faces.

column1: How many subsets does a set with \(n\) elements have?
column2: \(2^{n}\), including the empty set and the set itself.

column1: Describe the idea of floating-point round-off error.
column2: Real numbers are stored with finite precision, so most values are rounded to the nearest representable number, introducing a relative error bounded by machine epsilon.

column1: State the iteration formula for Newton's method.
Describe the bisection method and its convergence.
column2: On \([a, b]\) with \(f(a)\cdot f(b) < 0\), repeatedly halve the interval keeping the sign change; the root is bracketed and error halves each step (linear convergence).

column1: What is the fixed-point iteration for solving \(x = g(x)\)?
column2: \(x_{n+1} = g(x_n)\); it converges near a fixed point \(x^{*}\) if \(|g'(x^{*})| < 1\).

column1: State the trapezoidal rule for \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\) (single interval) and its error order.
column2: \(\approx \frac{b - a}{2} \cdot (f(a) + f(b))\); the composite error is \(O(h^{2})\).

column1: State Simpson's rule for \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\) and its error order.
column2: \(\approx \frac{b - a}{6} \cdot (f(a) + 4f(\tfrac{a+b}{2}) + f(b))\); the composite error is \(O(h^{4})\).

column1: Give the linear interpolation estimate of \(f(x)\) between points \((x_0, y_0)\) and \((x_1, y_1)\).
column2: \(f(x) \approx y_0 + \frac{y_1 - y_0}{x_1 - x_0} \cdot (x - x_0)\).

column1: What does the condition number of a problem measure?
column2: How sensitive the output is to small changes in the input; a large condition number means the problem is ill-conditioned.

column1: Define the order of convergence \(p\) of a sequence converging to \(x^{*}\).
column2: It is the value \(p\) for which \(|x_{n+1} - x^{*}| \approx C\cdot|x_n - x^{*}|^{p}\); \(p = 1\) is linear, \(p = 2\) is quadratic (e.g., Newton's method).
